items.title: GAO reports federal workforce declined by more than 11% in 2025
items.summary: A June 17 GAO report found the federal civilian workforce at major agencies fell by nearly 256,000 employees during 2025 following presidential directives.
items.details: The Government Accountability Office reported that nearly 378,000 employees separated from 22 major agencies in 2025, while about 127,000 were hired. The total workforce declined by more than 11% from December 2024 to January 2026, with declines ranging from about 1% at DHS to over 45% at the Department of Education.

items.title: State Department to add expedited 'premium' visa service
items.summary: The administration announced a premium expedited service for foreigners seeking business or tourist visas, priced at $750 on top of the standard fee.
items.details: Under the new option, applicants for business or tourist visas could pay $750 for expedited service, in addition to the existing $185 application fee. The move was reported as part of broader changes to visa and immigration processing.

items.title: Federal Reserve holds interest rate target range steady
items.summary: The Federal Open Market Committee directed that the federal funds rate be maintained in a target range of 3.5% to 3.75% effective June 18.
items.details: Following its June 16-17 meeting, the FOMC released a statement, implementation note, and projections. The committee directed operations to maintain the federal funds rate in a 3.5%-3.75% range and continue reinvesting principal payments from its securities holdings.

items.title: U.S. and Iran negotiate following signed memorandum of understanding
items.summary: U.S. and Iranian delegations held talks in Switzerland and agreed to a road map toward a final deal within 60 days under a memorandum of understanding signed June 17.
items.details: The memorandum of understanding, signed June 17 by President Trump and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian, extended a ceasefire by 60 days. Negotiators in Switzerland established working groups on sanctions, nuclear issues, reconstruction and monitoring, and a mechanism to address hostilities in Lebanon.

items.title: IAEA head says interim agreement provides for nuclear site supervision
items.summary: IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi said on June 26 that the U.S.-Iran memorandum indicates the nuclear portion will be supervised, amid differing accounts over inspections.
items.details: Grossi spoke in Tokyo as the U.S. and Iran offered differing public statements about whether Iran had agreed to allow inspectors to view its nuclear sites. He said the memorandum specifically indicates the nuclear part of the agreement will be supervised.

items.title: Israel and Lebanon sign framework agreement with U.S.
items.summary: Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on June 26 that Israel and Lebanon signed a framework agreement with the United States described as a first step toward peace.
items.details: The announcement came amid continued diplomatic activity in the region tied to the broader U.S.-Iran negotiations. The same day brought reports of renewed strikes and a vessel attack in the Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: Congress passes 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
items.summary: The House passed the housing affordability bill 358-32 on June 23 after the Senate approved it 85-5, sending the bipartisan measure toward the president's desk.
items.details: The legislation includes more than 50 provisions intended to remove barriers to building homes, lower housing costs, and shift more control to local governments. It was led by Senators Elizabeth Warren and Tim Scott and Representatives Maxine Waters and French Hill.

items.title: Signing ceremony for housing bill canceled
items.summary: President Trump canceled a planned signing ceremony for the housing bill on June 24, saying Congress must first pass a strict voter identification bill.
items.details: The bill had cleared both chambers with bipartisan support. The president stated his position on tying his action to passage of voter ID legislation.

items.title: Supreme Court allows administration to end Temporary Protected Status for Haiti and Syria
items.summary: In a 6-3 ruling in Mullin v. Doe on June 25, the Court cleared the way for the federal government to end TPS designations for Haiti and Syria.
items.details: The justices paused lower court rulings that had barred ending the designations. Writing for the majority, Justice Alito said the law generally bars courts from reviewing the Homeland Security Secretary's determinations to end a TPS designation. Justices Sotomayor, Kagan, and Jackson dissented.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on asylum 'turnback' policy in Mullin v. Al Otro Lado
items.summary: In a 6-3 decision June 25, the Court ruled that an asylum seeker standing in Mexico has not 'arrived in the United States' under federal immigration law.
items.details: The case addressed a now-defunct policy under which border officials blocked migrants from setting foot on U.S. soil. The majority opinion was delivered by Justice Alito; Justice Sotomayor, joined by Justices Kagan and Jackson, dissented.
items.source: Supreme Court of the United States

items.title: Supreme Court strikes Hawaii law on firearms on private property
items.summary: In a 6-3 decision in Wolford v. Lopez on June 25, the Court ruled states cannot require gun owners to get permission from property owners before bringing firearms onto their land.
items.details: The Court said requiring advance permission is an undue burden on the right to possess and carry a firearm. Five states had passed laws requiring such advance permission.

items.title: Supreme Court ruling in Louisiana v. Callais narrows Voting Rights Act
items.summary: The Court's 6-3 ruling reinterpreted Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, affecting how states draw majority-minority districts ahead of the 2026 midterms.
items.details: The majority held that states have broad freedom to draw district lines, including for partisan advantage, but that maps which maximize the electoral chances of non-white candidates can violate the equal-protection clause. Justice Alito wrote the majority opinion and Justice Kagan dissented.

items.title: Mid-decade redistricting battles continue across multiple states
items.summary: After 10 states enacted new congressional maps, analysts estimated Republicans could gain up to 16 seats and Democrats up to six heading into the fall.
items.details: Texas, North Carolina, Missouri, Ohio, Florida, Alabama and Louisiana enacted Republican-favored maps, while California and Virginia approved maps favoring Democrats and a Utah court ordered a new map. Several maps faced ongoing litigation as of June 2026.

CA.title: Newsom signs SB 73 restricting law enforcement access to ballots
CA.summary: Governor Gavin Newsom signed Senate Bill 73, which limits when law enforcement can access ballots, voter lists, and voting equipment, effective immediately ahead of the June 2 primary.
CA.details: The law criminalizes taking cast ballots from the custody of a local election official and prohibits warrantless search or seizure of voting machines and voter rolls. It followed an incident in which a county sheriff seized ballots from a registrar of voters.

SC.title: South Carolina House passes new congressional map
SC.summary: The South Carolina House passed a new congressional map 74-37 aimed at producing seven Republican-leaning districts and moving congressional primaries to August 18.
SC.details: The map redraws all seven districts, including the seat held by Rep. James Clyburn, and was advanced after a U.S. Supreme Court decision on Louisiana's map and a request from the White House. Four Republicans voted against the measure, which advanced to the state Senate.

IA.title: Gov. Reynolds signs final 2026 session bills, including limits on gubernatorial emergency powers
IA.summary: Governor Kim Reynolds signed the final bills of the 2026 session on June 2, including a measure barring governors from closing private businesses or places of worship during emergencies.
IA.details: House File 2694 also requires a governor to obtain legislative approval to change election laws during emergencies. Reynolds, who is not seeking another term, also signed bills on citizenship verification for educator licenses and state jobs.

CT.title: Gov. Lamont signs youth online safety and AI law
CT.summary: Governor Ned Lamont signed Public Act 26-15, a bipartisan measure addressing youth social media protections, artificial intelligence regulation, and workforce training.
CT.details: The law requires social media companies to verify users' ages and obtain parental permission before minors can access addictive algorithmic feeds. It was developed with the attorney general and the legislature's General Law committee co-chair.

VT.title: Gov. Scott signs comprehensive data privacy law
VT.summary: Governor Phil Scott signed S.71, the Vermont Data Privacy and Online Surveillance Act, on June 16, making Vermont one of the latest states with a comprehensive consumer privacy law.
VT.details: The law takes effect January 1, 2028, and grants enforcement authority to the state attorney general with no private right of action. Scott had vetoed a stricter version of the legislation in 2024.

NH.title: Gov. Ayotte signs 54 bills into law
NH.summary: Governor Kelly Ayotte announced she had signed 54 bills into law the week of June 19 while also issuing several vetoes.
NH.details: The signed bills covered topics including off-highway recreational vehicle operation, employer health benefit plan data access, and water system contamination mitigation. Ayotte issued veto statements on bills concerning surplus land sales and historical markers.

OH.title: Gov. DeWine signs bills into law
OH.summary: Governor Mike DeWine signed several bills on June 18, including the 'Keep Them Safe Act' and a measure creating the 'Joshua Alert' for missing children with autism or developmental disabilities.
OH.details: The bills included measures on construction contracts, unemployment compensation treatment of professional employer organizations, and a statewide emergency alert system for missing autistic or developmentally disabled children.

AZ.title: Gov. Hobbs vetoes AI-related bills
AZ.summary: Governor Katie Hobbs vetoed three AI-related bills passed by the legislature, among 88 bills she vetoed in a single day on June 19.
AZ.details: The vetoed measures included a chatbot safety bill and a bill directing state agencies to identify opportunities to implement AI systems. The Republican-majority House and Senate had approved the three AI bills earlier in June.

IL.title: New Illinois laws take effect July 1
IL.summary: A set of new Illinois laws takes effect July 1, including a suspension of the motor fuel tax inflation adjustment and an expanded definition of cyberbullying.
IL.details: Public Act 104-0468, signed in June 2026, suspends the Consumer Price Index adjustment for the motor fuel tax rate until 2027. Other laws address driver's license renewal for seniors and AI-related content in the school code.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources and does not endorse or oppose any party, official, or position.
